chunks.text: Not Just Pedigree Twelve Lessons From Good Dogs About Family Pressure and Freedom By Kyung Suk Yi and Jin Sung Kim © Kyung Suk Yi and Jin Sung Kim What makes you valuable? Is it your last name? Your parents’ jobs? The awards on your family’s wall? Or is it something deeper, something that has nothing to do with where you come from? Meet twelve dogs who know that pedigree doesn’t determine their worth. A shelter mutt can love just as fiercely as a champion show dog. A Lab doesn’t need the right friends to be happy. A German Shepherd can serve without following his family’s footsteps.

Through their stories, you’ll meet young (and not- so-young) people like you who are learning to navigate their family expectations, cultural traditions, and the weight of being “special.” You’ll discover that the pressure to be perfect, to carry on traditions, or to live up to your family name doesn’t have to define you. Because here’s the truth: God doesn’t check your pedigree before He loves you. 2 The proceeds from this book goes to: The Never Alone Mission ℅ A&K 440 N. Wolfe Rd. Sunnyvale, CA 94085

Front Cover Photo: Robot Photo unsplash.com/@robotphoto 3 For all the young (and not-so-young) people carrying the weight of heritage, may you find freedom in love. For all the families learning to hold tradition with grace, may you find peace. And for every reader who needed to hear that their worth isn’t tied to meeting every expectation, this book is for you. 4 Introduction 6 Lesson 1: The Perfect Playdate Pup 12 Lesson 2: The Expected Shepherd 18 Lesson 3: The Lonely Lab 24

We didn’t set out to write a book about family expectations. We were just paying attention to dogs. One of us, the deacon, has been living with dogs almost all his life. Purebreds with papers, mutts from the shelter, dogs with impressive lineages, dogs with unknown histories. Over the years, he started noticing something. The dogs with the finest pedigrees weren’t always the happiest. And the ones with no papers at all often showed the most joy. The other one of us, the layperson, has walked through the unique pressures of family heritage. The weight of cultural expectations. The unspoken requirements that come with certain last names, certain traditions, certain inherited dreams. In those experiences, clarity emerged about what truly matters and what merely seems important.

This book, the eighth in our Tails of Grace series, explores something we rarely talk about openly: the hidden struggles of those born into families with strong identities. Whether your family is 6 prominent in the community or simply prominent in your life, whether the expectations come from cultural heritage or family tradition, whether the pressure is public or private, these stories are for you. The stories in this book are based on real observations of dogs and real conversations with friends who’ve walked these paths. We’ve changed names, details, and circumstances to protect privacy, and sometimes we’ve woven together multiple experiences into single narratives. But the struggles, the emotions, and the breakthroughs are all true. They happened. To real families, real young people, real dogs trying to make sense of the expectations placed upon them.

Through twelve lessons, we’ll meet dogs and families navigating the complex terrain of inherited expectations. A Shiba Inu learning to be “perfect” whenever guests arrive. A German Shepherd whose rescue dog lineage doesn’t match his gentle temperament. A Border Collie trying to hold it together while her missionary family adjusts to a new country. A mixed breed whose companionship through a baseball injury teaches a family what really matters. 7 These aren’t just stories about dogs. They’re stories about us. About the young person who feels crushed under the weight of being a pastor’s kid. About the immigrant’s child carrying their parents’ sacrificed dreams. About the heir to a family business who dreams of art instead. About anyone who has ever wondered if they’re loved for who they are or for what they represent.

We’ve learned that dogs don’t read pedigree papers. They don’t care about bloodlines or breeding certificates. A shelter mutt will love you just as fiercely as a champion show dog. And perhaps more importantly, they teach us that worth isn’t determined by lineage. In the same way, God doesn’t check our family tree before offering His love. He doesn’t measure our value by how well we fulfill family expectations or cultural traditions. His love isn’t conditional on whether we become what our parents hoped or what our heritage demands.

If you’re picking up this book, perhaps you know what it’s like to carry expectations that feel heavier than they should. Maybe you’ve sat through family gatherings where your achievements are the primary topic of conversation. Maybe you’ve 8 wondered whether people see you or just your family name. Maybe you’ve felt guilty for wanting something different than what’s been planned for you. Or perhaps you’re a parent, grandparent, or mentor beginning to realize the weight your hopes might place on the young people you love. Maybe you’re starting to question whether the traditions you’ve held sacred are serving or suffocating the next generation.

Whoever you are, whatever brought you here, we want you to know: these struggles are real, they matter, and you’re not alone in them. The stories ahead won’t provide easy answers. Family dynamics, cultural heritage, and generational expectations are complex realities that can’t be solved in simple steps. But we hope these twelve lessons will offer something just as valuable: recognition, understanding, and the beginning of freedom. Because here’s what we’ve discovered: the very things that make family heritage beautiful, the traditions, values, stories, and dreams passed down through generations, can also become prisons

9 when they leave no room for individual calling. The love that wants the best for us can sometimes demand too much from us. But it doesn’t have to be this way. Families can honor their heritage while making space for new expressions of it. Parents can hold hopes for their children with open hands. Young people can respect where they come from while also embracing where they’re going. Love can exist without conditions. Worth can exist without performance. We know because we’ve seen it. In the families who finally learned to breathe. In the young people who found their own voice without losing their family. In the parents who discovered their children’s different paths weren’t rejections but expansions of family legacy.

And yes, we’ve seen it in dogs. In the purebred who plays with the mutt without checking papers. In the show dog who’s happiest rolling in mud. In the working dog who’d rather comfort than compete. They remind us that identity is more than inheritance, that joy is more than meeting expectations, that love is more than lineage. 10 So welcome to these twelve lessons. We pray they bring you hope, freedom, and the deep assurance that you are valued not for your pedigree, but for your heart.

A Shiba Inu lived in a household where appearances mattered. The family was well-known in their community, the kind of home where the doorbell rang frequently with visitors bearing polite smiles and carefully wrapped gifts. On an ordinary Tuesday afternoon, when the house was empty except for the family, the dog moved through the rooms with easy confidence. His tail curved naturally over his back in the breed’s typical curl. He carried his toys from room to room, dropping a squeaky hedgehog at the girl’s feet while she did homework, then trotting to the kitchen to check his water bowl. When he played in the backyard, his movements were loose and free. He sprinted suddenly across the grass, pounced at falling leaves, and shook his rope toy hard.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: The Pressure of Being "On" All the Time

chunks.text: The girl threw a tennis ball and the dog chased it with complete joy. He skidded on the grass, missed the catch entirely, and circled back with his mouth 12 open in what looked remarkably like a grin. He was simply a dog being a dog. Thursday afternoon brought visitors. The change began two hours before the doorbell would ring. The mother moved through the house with purposeful energy, straightening cushions and wiping already-clean counters. The dog’s water bowl was moved from its usual spot to a less visible corner. His toys were gathered up and placed in a basket that was then tucked into the laundry room.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: The Pressure of Being "On" All the Time

chunks.text: When the doorbell finally rang, the dog’s entire posture shifted. His tail dropped lower. His ears, which had been relaxed and moving, pulled slightly back. He approached the arriving guests with careful, measured steps. There was no enthusiastic bounding like his normal greetings. When the visitors bent to pet him, he held perfectly still, accepting their touch without leaning into it, without the full-body wiggle of genuine canine pleasure. 13 During the visit, the dog settled on his bed in the corner of the living room. He lay in an alert position with his front legs stretched out, head up, eyes tracking every movement in the room. It was the posture of alertness, not rest. His breathing was shallow and quick. When one of the visiting children approached with a cookie, the dog glanced toward the mother before carefully taking it. This was different from the eager, immediate acceptance he showed when the family offered treats.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: The Pressure of Being "On" All the Time

chunks.text: Most telling was what he did not do. He did not bring toys to the guests. He did not start playing with the visiting children. He did not perform any of the small, natural behaviors that marked his ordinary hours: the play bows, the excited spins, the gentle nose-nudges requesting attention. He had become a display version of himself, present but somehow absent. Even after the guests left, the tension remained in his body. For more than an hour, his ears stayed slightly flattened. His movements stayed controlled and careful. Only after dinner, when the family had settled into their evening routine, did his body language begin to soften. The tail curl
chunks.lesson_subtitle: The Pressure of Being "On" All the Time

chunks.text: 14 returned gradually. The muscles along his shoulders loosened. The pattern repeated with every visit. The dog had learned to read the signs: the extra cleaning, the rearrangement of his belongings, the particular energy in the house that came before guests. He had learned what was expected: perfect behavior, controlled responses, a demonstration of excellent training and breeding. The cost of this performance was visible in small ways. His appetite decreased on visiting days. His sleep became restless. The father noticed him shifting positions throughout the night, unable to settle into the deep sleep that usually made him unaware of household sounds. On mornings after particularly long visits, the dog moved slowly, as if tired despite getting enough rest.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: The Pressure of Being "On" All the Time

chunks.text: The girl began to notice these patterns. She watched her dog transform from a playful, expressive companion into something more like a well-behaved ornament. She saw how he checked for approval before every action when guests were present. His joy depended on whether he was being watched. His natural behavior disappeared under the weight of acting perfect for others. 15 One evening, after a particularly exhausting afternoon of visitors, she sat with the dog in the backyard. There was no one watching or judging. She threw the tennis ball into the growing darkness. Gradually, the dog began to play, slowly at first, then with increasing freedom. He played the way he played when no one was counting on him to be impressive.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: The Pressure of Being "On" All the Time

chunks.text: His body remembered what it felt like to move without thinking. He ran simply for the sake of running. He missed the ball and did not care. He was clumsy and joyful and completely, naturally himself. Watching him, the girl remembered something from the Bible. When the prophet Samuel went to choose Israel’s next king, he looked at the tall, impressive sons of Jesse and thought surely one of them must be God’s choice. But God told Samuel that people look at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: The Pressure of Being "On" All the Time

chunks.text: The girl understood then what she was seeing in her dog. Visitors judged him by his perfect behavior during their stay, by how well he performed and how impressive he appeared. But that performance was exhausting and false. The 16 real dog, the one with his true heart showing, only appeared when no one demanded anything of him. She wondered if people experienced the same thing. Perhaps they learned to perform for approval, showing only the version of themselves that others expected to see. Perhaps they became exhausted from constantly acting perfect, never able to simply be themselves without judgment.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: The Pressure of Being "On" All the Time

chunks.text: The contrast was clear: the dog performing for approval versus the dog simply being. One required constant effort and drained him of joy. The other was natural and restful. Her dog’s worth was not in his perfect sit-stay or his controlled greeting of guests. His value was in his actual self, the one that played and loved freely and sometimes missed the tennis ball entirely. That was the dog she loved. That was the heart that mattered. 17
chunks.lesson_subtitle: The Pressure of Being "On" All the Time

chunks.text: A German Shepherd was born into a famous family. His father had found survivors in collapsed buildings after earthquakes. His grandfather had located lost hikers in dangerous mountain terrain. The training facility kept a bronze statue of his grandfather near the entrance, and news crews had interviewed his father’s handlers multiple times. Everyone in the search and rescue unit knew the bloodline. From the day the puppy arrived at the training center, the handlers talked about his future as if it were already decided. They would pat his head and say things like, “Third generation rescue dog. He’s got it in his blood.” When he turned six months old, they enrolled him in the same intensive training program that had shaped his father and grandfather.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Identity Crisis: "Am I Called or Just Expected?"

chunks.text: The training began with sirens and rubble piles. The other puppies in his group bounded eagerly 18 toward the simulated disaster scenes, tails wagging as they climbed over concrete chunks and twisted metal. They barked excitedly when they found the hidden training dummies. Their bodies showed energy and enthusiasm. The German Shepherd’s body told a different story. When the sirens sounded, his ears flattened against his head. During the rubble exercises, he would freeze in place, his breathing fast and shallow. After intense training sessions, his body would tremble. He learned the commands quickly, which showed his intelligence. His body language during training showed something else: stress.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Identity Crisis: "Am I Called or Just Expected?"

chunks.text: His trainer noticed these patterns and started keeping careful notes. The dog’s appetite dropped on training days. He would retreat to his kennel after sessions and lie motionless for hours while the other dogs played and socialized. The veterinarian called it a chronic stress response. The trainer brought her concerns to the head of the unit. The supervisor dismissed them. “His father was nervous at first too,” he said. “It’s in his genetics. He just needs more time.” 19 The pressure continued. Everyone expected him to follow the family tradition. Everyone saw the famous bloodline and assumed the rest would follow naturally. Week after week, the training sessions brought the same results: stress signals, trembling, loss of appetite.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Identity Crisis: "Am I Called or Just Expected?"

chunks.text: Then the trainer decided to try something without permission. One morning, instead of bringing the dog to the training facility, she loaded him into her vehicle and drove to the hospital. The hospital was quieter than the training grounds. Instead of sirens and emergency commands, there were soft conversations and gentle beeping from medical equipment. The moment they entered the children’s section of the hospital, the dog’s body changed. The trembling stopped. His ears came forward in a relaxed position. His tail, which had been tucked during training for months, began a gentle wag.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Identity Crisis: "Am I Called or Just Expected?"

chunks.text: A young girl sat in a wheelchair in the hallway. She was small for her age and had lost her hair from chemotherapy treatments. She had been asking to see a dog all week. The dog approached her slowly and sat beside her wheelchair without being commanded to do so. He simply settled there, 20 calm and steady, allowing her to run her fingers through his fur. “He’s so peaceful,” the girl whispered to her mother. “I feel better.” For the next hour, the dog moved quietly from room to room. He seemed to know which children needed company. He would rest his head on a bed rail for one child, offer a gentle paw to another, or simply lie beside a third child who was afraid. He needed no commands. He was not performing. He was simply being what he naturally was.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Identity Crisis: "Am I Called or Just Expected?"

chunks.text: The hospital staff watched with amazement. The head nurse told the trainer that most therapy dogs took months to learn how to read a room this well. This kind of natural understanding was rare. The girl’s mother approached the trainer in the hallway. “Thank you for bringing him,” she said. “This is the first time I’ve seen her smile since her diagnosis. She has another treatment next week, and she’s been so afraid. But just now, she told me she thinks she can do it.”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Identity Crisis: "Am I Called or Just Expected?"

chunks.text: 21 Back at the training facility, the conversations were difficult. The supervisor reviewed the dog’s training records and admitted what everyone could see. “He’s not making it in search and rescue,” he said. “Maybe we need to accept that not every dog in this bloodline is meant for this work.” The trainer described what she had seen at the hospital. She explained how all the stress symptoms had disappeared, how naturally the dog had connected with the sick children, how his calm presence had brought comfort. She repeated what the girl’s mother had told her. “Giving a little hope and comfort to children going through pain and suffering. I think it’s just as important as pulling someone out from rubble. Don’t you?”
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Identity Crisis: "Am I Called or Just Expected?"

chunks.text: There was a long silence. Then the supervisor spoke. “His father served where his strengths could do the most good. We should do the same for this dog.” Six months later, the German Shepherd wore a different kind of badge. Instead of “Search and Rescue,” his vest read “Therapy Dog.” He spent his days moving quietly through hospital corridors, offering comfort to children facing their own kinds of emergencies. 22 The trainer thought about this often. She remembered a story from the Bible about when the prophet Samuel went to choose Israel’s next king. Everyone expected Samuel to pick one of the older, taller, more impressive sons. They looked like kings. They seemed like the obvious choice. But God told Samuel to look past the obvious qualifications and see what was really there. The youngest son, the one no one expected, turned out to be God’s choice.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Identity Crisis: "Am I Called or Just Expected?"

chunks.text: The dog’s father’s statue still stood at the training facility entrance. People still spoke respectfully about the legendary bloodline. The dog had honored his family, but not by following their exact path. He had honored them by serving where his actual gifts could help others. Sometimes the greatest calling is not doing what your family did, but discovering what you were made to do. His paws walked a different path than his father’s. That did not make the path less valuable. It made it his own.
chunks.lesson_subtitle: Identity Crisis: "Am I Called or Just Expected?"

chunks.text: Every morning at seven fifteen, a chocolate Labrador Retriever watched through floor-to- ceiling windows as neighborhood dogs walked past with their owners. His tail would start wagging hopefully. He would press his nose against the glass, leaving fog marks that someone would clean later. The golden retriever from three houses down always tried to pull toward their driveway. The beagle across the street would bark a greeting. The Lab could only watch. The family lived in a gated community where houses had security systems and privacy fences. The father’s work involved high-level business deals. The mother’s career put her in the news regularly. Their son attended a private school forty minutes away. The family had reasons to be careful about who came near their home.

Labs are naturally social dogs. They were bred to work alongside people, to retrieve waterfowl for hunters, to be part of a team. Their friendly nature 24 is not just personality. It is built into what they are. This Lab had all those instincts, all that need for connection, and nowhere to put it. When children came to visit, the Lab’s tail would start wagging with excitement. He would bring his rope toy to them, ready to play. He wanted to play tug-of-war, to wrestle, to run. But the children had been given instructions by their parents. Be polite. Mind your manners. This is an important family. The children would pet him carefully and formally, then turn their attention elsewhere. The Lab would gradually retreat to his bed, confused about why these interactions felt wrong.

The boy noticed this pattern. He watched his dog press against the sliding door when children played in the backyard, whimpering to join them. He watched the confusion in the Lab’s eyes when another polite, careful visit ended and the dog returned to his bed. One evening, the boy found his parents reviewing security footage from a recent gathering. “He’s not okay,” he said. His mother looked up. “What do you mean?” 25 “He used to be so excited when people came over. Now when kids visit, he brings his toy to them, but they just pet him politely and then ignore him. So he goes back to his bed. His trainer even mentioned it last week. She said he doesn’t have the energy Labs usually have.”

The mother had noticed it too. The Lab’s natural enthusiasm had dimmed. “I know you worry about who comes near us, Mom,” the boy continued. “But keeping him away from everyone is making him sad. Today he sat at the back door watching the kids play for almost an hour. He was whimpering.” “What are you suggesting?” she asked. “Let’s go to the regular dog park. Just once. We don’t have to tell anyone who we are. I can say you work in business and law. That’s vague enough, right? We can go to one in a different city even. People won’t know us there.”

His parents looked at each other, having a whole conversation without words. Finally, they agreed to try. 26 The next Saturday, they drove thirty minutes to a public dog park in a neighboring town. The mother wore sunglasses and a baseball cap. The father stayed in the car initially, ready to leave quickly if needed. The moment they entered the double gates, the Lab transformed. His tail went into full speed. He bounded toward a group of dogs. There were mutts and purebreds, old dogs and young dogs, all playing together. For the first hour, the mother stood stiffly by the fence, watching for problems. But something else caught her attention.

The Lab was really playing. He wrestled with a pit bull mix. He raced a border collie. He shared a tennis ball with a three-legged rescue dog. His whole body showed joy. The boy was talking to other children there, comparing notes about their dogs, laughing about funny things the dogs did. He was being a regular kid. An older woman approached the mother. “Your Lab is beautiful,” she said. “They are such social souls.” 27 The mother started to give the prepared story they had practiced, but the woman stopped her. “Don’t worry. I’m not asking for your life story.” She smiled gently. “Watching happy dogs. That makes me happy, too.”

The woman’s kindness surprised her. She expected nothing in return, asked no questions about careers or connections. “We are new to this,” the mother admitted. “Dog parks? Oh, you will love it. Best community you will find. We all come here for the same reason. Our dogs need this. Everything else doesn’t really matter here.” Over the following weeks, Saturday mornings at the public park became part of the family’s routine. The father eventually started joining them. At first he worked on his phone from a bench. Gradually he began to chat with other dog owners about simple topics: the weather, dog breeds, training tips.

The Lab’s transformation was clear. His appetite improved. He played with his toys again. That smile that Labs are known for returned. The boy made friends too. Kids who knew him as “the boy 28 with the chocolate Lab,” not as the son of prominent parents. The family still kept their security measures. They still managed their public image carefully. But they had learned something important. Protecting their family did not mean complete isolation. That Sunday at church, the boy thought about a story from the Bible. Jesus met a woman at a well in the middle of the day. She came at that hour to avoid other people. She had been judged by her community, defined by her mistakes, seen only through the lens of her failures. But Jesus saw her differently. He talked to her about real things. He offered her something genuine: living water, true acceptance, being known for who she actually was.

The boy understood something then. The Lab had been isolated not because of anything wrong with him, but because of the family’s circumstances. Other dogs at the park did not care about security concerns or business deals or news coverage. They just wanted to play. And in that simple acceptance, the Lab had found what he needed. To be seen as what he really was: a friendly dog who loved to run and wrestle and share tennis balls. 29 Sometimes being truly known means stepping outside the protected spaces. It means risking genuine connection instead of staying safe behind windows. It means letting others see you for what you are, not what your family name suggests you should be.

The Lab’s tail still wagged at the window on weekday mornings. But now he knew that Saturday was coming. He knew there was a place where Labs could be Labs, where children could be children, where everyone came together for the same simple reason. Their dogs needed this. And so did they. 30

A golden retriever mix lived with a family in a quiet suburban neighborhood. The father worked as the principal of the local elementary school. Parents throughout the community knew him as someone who cared deeply about children and their education. The mother ran a family counseling practice where she helped parents work through difficult situations with their teenagers. Their son was in his junior year of high school. The trouble started small. The dog began hiding under the boy’s bed during thunderstorms. He would tremble quietly instead of seeking comfort like he used to. When the dishwasher ran its loud cycle, he would slip away to the back bedroom closet. Even the sound of the garage door opening would send him retreating to dark, quiet spaces.

The boy noticed first. “Mom, something’s wrong with him,” he said one evening after finding the dog under the bed in one of the bedrooms. “He’s acting really weird.” 31 His mother watched the dog’s behavior over the next few days. The dog still wagged his tail when family members came home. He still ate his meals and went for walks. But there were changes. He startled more easily. He avoided rooms where people were talking loudly. He had developed a habit of checking escape routes wherever he went.

“Maybe he needs to see the vet,” the father suggested. “Could be an ear infection making him sensitive to sound.” At the veterinary clinic, the doctor examined the dog thoroughly but found nothing physically wrong. “His hearing is fine,” she reported. “This looks more like anxiety. Has anything changed at home recently? Any scary events?” The family exchanged puzzled looks. Their household ran smoothly. Regular meals, consistent routines, family time each evening. They could not think of any major changes or traumatic incidents.

But the boy remembered something. “There was that really bad storm last month,” he said. “The one that knocked out power for three days. He was terrified the whole time.” 32 The veterinarian nodded. “Sometimes a single scary event can trigger ongoing anxiety, especially in sensitive animals. The good news is this is treatable, but it will take patience and consistency.” She explained how noise anxiety worked and gave them a treatment plan. They would start with gradual exposure to sounds. The dog needed safe spaces where he could go when frightened. Medication was possible if needed. “The important thing,” she said, “is not to hide or ignore the problem. Anxiety gets worse when animals try to handle it alone in the dark.”

Over the next few weeks, the family worked on the treatment plan. They created a quiet safe room where the dog could go during storms or loud noises. They played recordings of thunder at very low volumes, gradually increasing the sound over time. They rewarded calm behavior and never forced him to face scary situations before he was ready. But the father seemed uncomfortable with the whole process. One evening, after a neighbor asked why their dog seemed different lately, he brought it up with his wife. The father had always been a

33 private person, mostly because he was sensitive to what others might be thinking of him and his family. “I don’t like sharing personal issues,” he said. “I’d like it if we handled it quietly.” “It’s just anxiety,” the mother said. “Lots of dogs have it.” “I know, but…” The father hesitated. “Won’t people wonder if there’s something wrong with how we care for him? And that might affect how they see you as a counselor.” The boy had been quiet during this conversation, but something about it bothered him. He watched his dog sitting calmly in his safe room, no longer frantically hiding behind furniture. The difference was clear. Because they were honest about the problem, they were able to get it diagnosed and treated properly. That was why their dog was getting better.

That weekend, one of the veterinarian’s friends stopped by to return some books she borrowed. She brought her teenage daughter with her. When a small thunderstorm rolled through that afternoon, the dog walked calmly to his safe room instead of hiding in panic. 34 “He’s working through some anxiety,” the boy said simply. “We’re helping him learn that it’s okay to be scared sometimes.” The visiting girl smiled. “That’s really cool that you’re helping him through it.” After they left, the boy found his parents in the kitchen. His heart was pounding. Watching his dog get better had given him the courage to speak up.

“I need to tell you something,” he began. “I’ve been dealing with something too. Like him. With anxiety.” He took a breath. “I’ve been having panic attacks. I can’t sleep. I keep thinking about what people will say if they find out our family isn’t perfect.” The kitchen fell silent. His father’s expression shifted from surprise to concern to something that looked like recognition. “How long has this been going on?” his mother asked gently. “A few months. But I didn’t want to say anything because…” The boy gestured helplessly. “You’re

35 both supposed to be the experts on this stuff. Dad helps kids with problems all day. Mom, you’re literally a counselor. I thought you’d be disappointed if your own son needed help.” The father sat down heavily. “Oh. I’m so sorry. Have we made you feel like you couldn’t come to us?” “Not on purpose,” the boy said. “But when you were worried about people judging us over his anxiety, I figured my problems would be even worse for your reputations.”

His mother reached across the table and took his hand. “Your wellbeing is infinitely more important than our professional image.” “But what if people find out?” the boy asked. The father was quiet for a long moment. Then he spoke slowly. “You know what? Let them. If we can’t be honest about our own family’s struggles, how can I expect the families at school to trust me with theirs? You help other families work through these exact issues,” he said, looking at his wife. “Maybe it’s time we practiced what we preach.”

36 The boy thought about a story from the Bible. In the book of John, Jesus said something important about light and darkness. He said that people who do evil hide in darkness because they do not want their deeds exposed. But those who live by the truth come into the light. The passage was not saying that struggles or problems are evil. It was saying that hiding things in darkness gives them more power. Bringing them into the light is where healing begins.

Their dog had taught them this lesson. When he tried to hide his fear alone in dark spaces, the anxiety grew worse. When the family brought the problem into the light, acknowledged it honestly, and worked on it together, healing became possible. A few weeks later, the veterinarian’s friend visited again with her daughter. When thunder started, the dog walked to his safe room. The visiting girl watched and smiled. “He’s doing so much better,” she said. The boy nodded.

The boy watched him as their dog emerged from his safe room later that evening. He was moving calmly instead of frantically. Their willingness to be honest about their struggles was what made it 37 possible for him to start healing. The family’s openness about their struggles could help other families feel less alone with theirs. Sometimes the bravest thing we can do is stop hiding. Sometimes the strongest families are not the ones who never struggle, but the ones who face their struggles honestly, together, in the light.

Bori stopped on the path. Her ears turned toward a sound she didn’t know. Singing came from the temple two streets away. Monks in orange robes were gathering for evening prayers. Before Bori could figure out that sound, motorcycles roared past the gate: one, then another, then three more. The fruit seller’s voice blasted from his speaker. He was talking about mangoes. Bori had heard it a hundred times, but she still jumped. The Australian Shepherd walked along the fence. Her blue and gray coat looked dull. She had been in Chiang Mai for a while now, and every sound still scared her. Every smell was wrong. Every day was different.

She stopped at the corner where she’d been digging that morning. Her paws hurt from scratching at the hard ground under the dirt. Back in Los 39 Angeles, she’d had a job. She kept the pastor’s daughter safe on mountain trails. She watched for loose rocks. She made sure the girl stayed on the path. Here, the girl went into a small room with a Thai woman every afternoon. Bori had to stay outside. She had no job anymore. Thunder rumbled. The rain came almost every afternoon. Bori’s whole body got tight. She was already panting in the thick air. She ran to the covered porch where the family ate meals.

The daughter sat at the table with her Thai workbook. She said words quietly to herself. The pastor’s wife cut vegetables in the outdoor kitchen. She moved slowly, like she was tired. The pastor was gone. He was gone most days, traveling to villages in the mountains. The thunder got louder. Bori pushed herself against the wall. She whimpered. The girl looked up. “Bori’s shaking again.” 40 The mother glanced at the dog but said nothing. She’d been trying to cut a papaya for ten minutes. The knife was too dull. The fruit was too soft.

Bori knew what dinner time used to mean. The girl would do homework at the kitchen table. The mother would cook in a real kitchen with an oven and a stove. Familiar smells would fill the air, and Bori knew they meant dinner was coming. The pastor would come home at six. Always at six. Everyone would eat together. Then Bori would run in the backyard while the girl threw tennis balls against the fence. Here, sounds never stopped. The city never slept. Bori had no backyard to run in. Every day was different. She couldn’t predict what would happen next. And the family seemed as lost as she was.

Heavy rain started suddenly. Bori’s whimper turned into panting. Her mouth hung open. Spit dripped. Her heart pounded. She couldn’t breathe right in the thick air. “Something’s really wrong with her,” the daughter said. She closed her workbook. 41 The mother wiped her hands. She came over and bent down by the dog. Bori’s eyes were wild. Her pupils were huge. “She’s having a panic attack.” The girl touched Bori’s head gently. The dog felt hot. Too hot. “She’s been like this for weeks, Mom. Since we got here.”

The mother sat back. She looked at Bori. The dog was scared. Something in her own chest felt tight. That evening, the pastor came home from the mountains. His daughter sat on the porch with Bori pressed against her legs. The rain had stopped. Everything was still wet. The air felt heavy with water and the smell of wet dirt. “How was the trip?” she asked. “Good. The village elder wants to talk more about bringing a teacher up for the children.” He set down his backpack. Mud covered it from the mountain roads. “Where’s your mother?”

“Lying down. She said she had a headache.” 42 He frowned. Headaches happened a lot now. He looked at Bori. The dog was panting even though the air was cooler. “Is the dog sick?” “Dad, look at her. Really look.” He bent down and looked at Bori. For weeks, he had only noticed she was there. Now he really watched her. Her coat was dull. Patches showed where she’d been licking herself. Her ribs stuck out. Her eyes looked crazy. They kept moving, looking for danger that wasn’t there. She couldn’t settle down, even when his daughter pet her head.

“How long has she been like this?” “Since shortly after we moved here. She barely eats. She digs holes by the fence. She shakes during every storm. Mom says she can hear her walk around all night.” The pastor had been busy. He had been learning Thai, meeting with village leaders, and talking with the hill tribes. He hadn’t noticed his dog was falling apart. 43 “Australian Shepherds are herding dogs. Maybe she just needs more exercise.” “Dad, she needs a job. She needs to know what she’s supposed to do. She needs the same things to happen every day.” His daughter’s voice broke a little. “Just like the rest of us.”

The words hung in the thick air. He looked at his daughter and really looked at her. He saw how tight her shoulders were. She looked tense, barely holding herself together. “What do you mean?” She kept petting Bori’s ears. She didn’t look at her father. “I’m scared all the time. At my Thai lessons, I’m afraid of saying something wrong. My teacher is really nice. But people watch us everywhere we go. They know we’re the pastor’s family. If I mess up, it makes you look bad. It makes the ministry look bad.”

He sat down on the step beside her. “You don’t have to be perfect.” “Yes, I do. That’s how it works, right? Everyone watches. Everyone judges. And now we’re here. I 44 don’t understand half of what people say. Everything is different. I can’t even read the street signs.” She was crying now. “I practice Thai for three hours every night. I’m so afraid of making you look bad.” “Three hours? After your lesson?” She nodded. “I can’t sleep anyway. The sounds here are so different. The temple bells. The little lizards clicking in the walls. Dogs barking all night. I lie there thinking about all the ways I might mess up.”

The pastor felt something break in his chest. He’d been so focused on his work, on his calling, on bringing the gospel to villages that had never heard it. He’d stopped seeing his family. He was quiet for a moment. Then he asked, “How is your mother really doing?” His daughter looked at the little house. “She cried for a long time yesterday. After she tried to buy food at the market. She thought she was asking for chicken. But she used the wrong tone. The vendor started laughing and couldn’t stop. Mom just left everything and came home.”

45 He stood up. He went inside. He found his wife lying in the dark. It was still early. He sat on the edge of the bed. She didn’t turn toward him. “I can’t do this.” He was quiet. He didn’t ask what she meant. He already knew. “Any of it,” she continued. “I can’t cook with food I don’t know. I can’t shop with people laughing at me. I can’t help our daughter with homework. The Thai workbook looks like squiggles. I can’t talk to our neighbors. I don’t speak the language. I can’t even call my mother. She gave up so much to help us get here. I can’t do anything.”

“…” “And I know this is God’s call for you. I know it matters. So I just keep trying. But I’m drowning. I can’t sleep. I can’t eat. I spent so long today looking at a bottle. I was trying to figure out if it was dish soap or laundry soap. I can’t read Thai letters. I just stared at that bottle forever.” The room was quiet. Lizards clicked on the walls. A neighbor’s radio played Thai music. 46 He thought about his day. He had visited three villages, met with elders, and planned Bible studies. He had felt good about how doors were opening. And while he did that, his wife couldn’t read a soap bottle. His daughter was too scared to speak. His dog was falling apart.

“I didn’t know,” he said. “I didn’t see it.” “You’ve been so busy with work.” “So busy I stopped seeing my own family.” He stood. “Come outside. Please.” They found their daughter still on the porch with Bori. The dog was digging at the concrete with her front paws. She dug over and over. The motion never stopped. Her nails were worn down. But she kept digging. She couldn’t stop. He watched the dog for a long time. “She’s really suffering.”

“She’s like the rest of us,” his wife said quietly. “We’re all lost. We’re all scared. We’re all always worried.” 47 Their daughter stood up. “Herding dogs need to know what they’re supposed to do. They need jobs. Back home, Bori’s job was keeping me safe on hikes. Here, she doesn’t have a job. She doesn’t know what she is expected to do. Every day is different. She can’t control anything.” His wife added, “And maybe that’s all of us. I don’t know what I’m supposed to be doing here. Our daughter is terrified of messing up. She practices Thai for hours every night because she’s so afraid of being embarrassed. We are trying to figure out the rules, but it’s not easy. We’re all trying to do the best we can, but we keep messing up. And we’re so tired.”

The pastor looked at his family. He saw his worried daughter, his overwhelmed wife, and his scared dog. He’d been so focused on reaching the villages that he hadn’t seen his own family falling apart right in front of him. “I’m canceling my trip next week,” he said. “I need to be here. I need to help.” Over the next few days, they started to change things. They began talking to each other about 48 everything. Not just the big struggles, but all the small daily ones they’d been handling alone.

The pastor called another missionary family. They were Korean Christians who had been in northern Thailand for several years. On a video call, the other wife listened and nodded as they explained what was happening. “The first year… it was hard. Really hard. It almost broke us. Our daughter was anxious all the time and our son barely ate anything. Our dog started acting weird, too.” “What did you do?” the pastor’s wife asked. “We had to figure out routines we could control. Same breakfast time. Same lesson time. Same family walk time. No matter what else happened. We gave our dog jobs again. You know how herding dogs are. They fall apart without routines. We let our kids know that it’s okay to struggle. That struggling is not a bad thing. We told our supporting churches we were having a hard time.” She paused. “That last one was hardest. Missionaries are supposed to be strong, right?”

“How did they respond?” 49 “Some were nice. Some were disappointed that we weren’t trusting God more. But the people who matter understood.” The pastor’s wife nodded slowly. “It helped to just admit we were struggling. We didn’t have to pretend everything was okay.” After the call, they sat together on the porch. Bori was at their daughter’s feet. She was still panting and restless. “What if we told our prayer partners this is harder than we expected?” the pastor’s wife said.

The pastor was quiet. All his updates had been positive. He had told stories about villages welcoming them, about people who wanted to learn, and about how God was helping. To admit struggle felt like admitting he’d failed. It felt like proving the people who questioned their call were right. But then he looked at Bori. The dog was panting even though it was cool. Her eyes kept moving. They never settled. She was always looking for the next danger.

50 He hadn’t known his family was struggling this badly. But now he did. He could keep sending positive updates to his supporters. Or he could tell them the truth. “Yes,” he said. “Let’s tell them the truth.” They started small. They established morning and evening routines that didn’t change. They would have breakfast at seven-thirty, family dinner at six, and bedtime at ten. They gave Bori a new job. They bought saddlebags that sat on her back with a pouch on each side. Every morning, the pastor’s wife would put the bags on Bori. Then they would walk to the market together with the neighbor. Bori would carry light items back in the pouches.

At first, Bori tried to shake the bags off. She walked stiffly. But the pastor’s wife was patient. She put the bags on Bori every morning at the same time. She said the same words. “Time for market, Bori. Let’s go.” After a few days, Bori stopped trying to remove the bags. When the bags came out, she would wait by the door. She had a routine she could count on. 51 The neighbor was an older Thai woman who went to the small international church. She didn’t speak English and the pastor’s wife’s Thai was still terrible, but they were patient with each other. The neighbor helped her learn.

The neighbor laughed with her at her mistakes, not at her. When she asked if cucumbers were spicy instead of asking how much they cost, the neighbor smiled. She pointed to the mild peppers. Then to the cucumbers. She helped her learn the difference. Their daughter stopped studying Thai for three hours every night. Instead, she studied for one hour. Then she spent time writing in a journal about what was hard, what was scary, and what she missed about home. With her permission, the pastor read her entries sometimes. He learned more about his daughter than he’d known in years.

Bori’s changes came slowly. The morning market trips gave her something to count on. After a couple weeks, she stopped digging at the ground. After a few more weeks, she started eating regular meals instead of stuffing herself one day and refusing food the next. She still jumped at strange 52 sounds. But she didn’t walk around all night anymore. She learned to settle down. One evening a month later, they sat together. They watched the sunset over the compound walls. The temple bells rang for evening prayers. Bori’s ears just flicked toward the sound. She didn’t flinch. She lay at their daughter’s feet. For the first time since they’d come to Thailand, her breathing was calm and even.

“She looks different,” the girl said. “So do all of you,” the pastor said. He thought about the email he’d sent last week. He had admitted that changing cultures was harder than expected. He had told them his family was struggling. He had asked for prayer not just for ministry success, but for making it through each day in a place so different from home. Several supporters had written back. They shared their own stories about struggling. They talked about times when they could barely keep going.

His wife put her hand on his shoulder. “It’s still hard.” 53 “Yes.” He nodded. The ministry was slower than he’d hoped. He’d had to say no to some travel so he could be home for family dinners. He’d told village elders he was still learning, still making mistakes, and still figuring out how to live in a culture that wasn’t his own. But his family was getting better. And that, he was learning, was ministry too. It was maybe the most important kind.

Their daughter scratched behind Bori’s ears. The dog’s eyes were half-closed. She looked peaceful in a way she hadn’t been for months. Somewhere in the city, someone set off firecrackers. Bori’s ears went up. But she didn’t panic. She listened. She checked. Then she settled again. “Good girl,” the daughter whispered. “You’re learning. We all are.” The temple bells rang again. This time, they didn’t sound like an invasion. They sounded more like a reminder. They were living in a real place with real

A family lived in a modest house, where baseball was more than a sport. It was a lifeline to a better future. Their seventeen-year-old son had been throwing fastballs since he could hold a ball. By high school, scouts from colleges regularly showed up at his games. Their Border Collie, Scout, had grown up alongside the son’s baseball career. Every afternoon for years, Scout chased the balls the boy threw against their backyard fence, returning them with the precision of an outfielder. The dog never failed to retrieve a ball, no matter how many throws they did.

The father worked two jobs: morning shifts at a warehouse and evening work as a janitor at the local community college. The mother cleaned offices downtown. Every dollar they saved went toward their son’s equipment, travel team fees, and private coaching sessions. The walls of their living 56 room displayed his trophies, newspaper clippings, and photos with college scouts. “Mijo, remember what the coach said about your slider,” the father would remind his son at breakfast. “The scout will be at Friday’s game.”

The mother kept a notebook tracking every statistic: batting average, earned run average, strikeouts per inning. She could recite the numbers from recent seasons without looking at her notes. At family gatherings, relatives would ask about the boy’s latest achievements before asking about his wellbeing. The boy never complained about the early morning workouts or the weekend tournaments far away. He understood what his success meant to his family. His younger sister had dreams of becoming a nurse, and a baseball scholarship would mean their parents could help pay for her education too.

Scout developed his own rituals around the games. He would sit perfectly still beside the mother in the stands, his body tense whenever the boy took the mound. After losses, Scout would bring the 57 boy his favorite tennis ball repeatedly as soon as they got home. The pressure intensified during the boy’s junior year. College recruitment was reaching its peak. Every game, every at-bat, every pitch mattered. The father started taking unpaid time off work to attend more games, despite the financial strain. The mother created spreadsheets comparing her son’s stats to other boys who were being recruited.

“Another boy got a full ride to a top program,” she mentioned one evening. “His pitching stats were only a bit better than yours last season.” The boy nodded, pushing his food around his plate. He had already done his morning run and had a bullpen session scheduled after dinner. His shoulder had been aching for weeks, but he had not mentioned it. The regional championship was coming up, and scouts from his dream schools had confirmed they would attend.

Scout would nose at the boy’s right shoulder when they sat together, whimpering softly. But the boy would gently push Scout away and grab his glove for another practice session. 58 The breaking point came during the regional semifinal. The boy was pitching a shutout, his fastball hitting hard and fast. A head scout sat directly behind home plate, radar gun in hand. Later in the game, he wound up for another fastball. The crack wasn’t the ball hitting the catcher’s mitt. It was something in his shoulder.

The ball sailed wild. The boy crumpled on the mound, gripping his shoulder. Scout, who had been sitting with the family, began barking frantically, straining against his leash. The stadium fell silent except for Scout’s desperate cries. The diagnosis was a torn rotator cuff. Surgery would be required. The doctor said the boy might pitch again after months of rehabilitation, but his velocity would likely never fully return. The dreams of college scholarships evaporated in that medical office. That evening, the house was quieter than it had ever been. The father sat at the kitchen table, staring at the bills that he had been putting off. The mother’s statistics notebook sat untouched on the table. She stared at it for a long moment, then put it in a drawer. Their son lay on the couch,

59 Scout pressed against his side, the dog’s head resting on his chest. “I’m sorry,” the boy whispered, tears streaming down his face. “I’m so sorry I let you down.” His grandmother, who had been staying with them during the tournament, sat in her corner chair with her worn Spanish Bible. She had been reading the same passage over and over since they had returned from the hospital. “Mijo,” she said softly, “listen to this. ‘Man looks at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart.’” She closed the Bible gently. “God doesn’t need you to be a star to love you. Neither do we.”

The boy buried his face in Scout’s fur, his shoulders shaking. The father looked up from the bills. His son lay there injured and heartbroken, apologizing as if this was his failure. “Mijo,” the father said, moving to sit beside his son. “You have nothing to apologize for.” 60 The mother joined them, running her hand through her son’s hair the way she did when he was small. Tears flowed silently down her cheeks as she looked at her boy in pain.

Over the next several weeks, as the boy began physical therapy, he moved the trophies to his room. The mother found her old guitar in the closet and started playing again in the evenings. The father began teaching his son about car repair, something they had never had time to do before. Scout adapted too. Instead of bringing balls for the boy to throw, he walked slowly beside him during their walks together. One afternoon, the sister found her brother sitting in the backyard with Scout.

“You know what’s weird?” the boy said. “I actually feel relieved. Like I can breathe for the first time in years.” The sister sat down beside him. “Mom and Dad seem different too. Yesterday, Dad asked me about my biology project. He wanted to know what I was learning.” 61 The boy ended up attending the community college where his father worked, qualifying for free tuition because his father worked there. He discovered a love for sports medicine, inspired by the physical therapists who helped him heal. He earned a scholarship that paid for some of his books and supplies. He had kept his grades up through all those years of baseball.

The father would sometimes find himself watching his son study at the kitchen table. “You know what, mijo?” he’d say. “I think this is exactly where you’re supposed to be.” 62 A Pause for Breath If you’ve made it this far, you might need a moment to breathe. We understand. These first six stories aren’t easy ones. You’ve read about dogs who learned to perform on command and families who forgot how to simply be together. You’ve seen young people carrying burdens they never asked for, trying to meet standards someone else set for them.

Maybe you recognized yourself in one of these stories. Maybe in several. Or maybe you’re holding this book because someone you love is struggling, and you’re only beginning to understand why. Wherever you are, we want you to know that you’re not alone in what you’re feeling. Dogs know something about rest. After they’ve been running hard or working all day, they just stop. They find a spot, lie down, and close their eyes. They don’t feel guilty about needing a break.

63 So take one now if you need to. Sit with what you’ve read. Let it settle. The journey isn’t over. In Part Two, the stories go deeper. Real healing often requires looking at wounds we’ve been trying to hide, and that’s where we’re headed next. Lesson 7: The Invisible Child Lesson 8: The Burden Bearer Lesson 9: The Guard Who Wandered Lesson 10: The Dog Who Left the Pack Lesson 11: The Soldier Who Couldn’t Rest Lesson 12: The Hunter Who Let Go

You’ll meet young people who feel invisible in their own families. You’ll sit with the “good child” who’s exhausted from always having to hold it together. You’ll walk alongside those who feel guilty for being human, for having limits, for needing help. But you’ll also find your way toward something we’ve all been searching for: freedom. We’re not talking about running away from family or rejecting your heritage. This freedom lets you stay connected to the people you love while still

64 having room to breathe. It’s learning that you can honor where you came from and still become who you’re meant to be. And somewhere along the way, you might find that the people who love you don’t actually need you to be perfect. They can love you even when you fall short of what they hoped for. The dogs in those next stories will help us find it. We hope you’ll join us there. 65

We are just a regular person and a local church deacon. The only thing truly worth mentioning is this: we’ve been forgiven. God has given us His Spirit to walk through this life and to slowly, steadily become more like Jesus. He gave His life for us on the cross, and in doing so, gave us life, now and forever. Jesus is our Savior, our Lord, and the reason we get out of bed every morning. 66
